# Raw research: 01 — AI credibility crisis (scale, speed, evidence)

**Prompt:** `docs/build/research/prompts/01-ai-credibility-crisis.md`  
**Research date:** 2026-04-12  
**Methods:** Web retrieval of primary sources (Pew, Ahrefs, NewsGuard, arXiv/DOI), industry reports, EU DisinfoLab, ATLA theological literature. Semantic Scholar API was rate-limited (429) during this session; arXiv API succeeded for one key paper.

---

## 1. “68% of internet users struggle to distinguish human-created from AI-generated content”

### Trace / origin

- **Targeted literature and news scan:** No reputable primary source was located that states **exactly 68%** of *internet users* “struggle to distinguish” AI vs. human content using that wording.
- **Close, verifiable relatives (U.S.-centric):**
  - **Pew Research Center (2025):** Among U.S. adults surveyed June 9–15, 2025 (n=5,023, American Trends Panel, weighted), **76%** say it is *extremely or very important* to be able to tell whether pictures, videos, and text were made by AI or people; **53%** say they are *not too* or *not at all confident* they can detect AI vs. human authorship.  
    - Source: [How Americans View AI and Its Impact on People and Society](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2025/09/17/how-americans-view-ai-and-its-impact-on-people-and-society/) (section “Detecting content created by AI versus humans”).  
    - Methodology: [AI impact on people and society — methodology](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2025/09/17/ai-impact-on-people-society-methodology/).  
    - Questionnaire/topline PDFs linked from that page (dated 2025-09-17).
  - **All About Cookies (2024):** Survey of **1,000 U.S. adults** via Prolific, **February 2024**. **77%** say they have “encountered something online that they believed to be from a real person only to later learn that it was generated by artificial intelligence.”  
    - Source: [Most Internet Users Have Been Fooled by AI-Generated Content Online [Survey]](https://allaboutcookies.org/ai-content-online-survey) (methodology at bottom of page).

### Confidence assessment — Movemental “68%” claim

| Claim variant | Assessment | Notes |
|---------------|--------------|-------|
| Exact “68% / internet users / struggle to distinguish” | **Unverified** | No matching primary citation found in this research pass. |
| “Most people lack confidence they can spot AI content” (U.S.) | **Verified (high confidence)** | Pew: **53%** not confident (see above). |
| “Large majorities have been misled at least once” (U.S. self-report) | **Partially supported** | All About Cookies: **77%** self-report at least one mistaken attribution; **not** a controlled discrimination task. |

**Recommendation for argument index:** Replace or footnote “68%” with Pew’s **53% not confident** + **76% importance**, or All About Cookies **77% fooled** with explicit U.S. scope, survey firm, and date—or **remove the number** until a primary source for 68% is produced.

---

## 2. “40–60% of blog posts on major platforms are AI-generated or AI-assisted (2025)”

### Best available prevalence proxies (methodologies differ)

1. **Ahrefs — new English web pages (April 2025), proprietary detector “bot_or_not”**  
   - **900,000** newly detected pages (one page per domain).  
   - **74.2%** of pages contained *some* model-detected AI-generated content.  
   - Breakdown: **2.5%** “pure AI,” **25.8%** “pure human,” **71.7%** mix; within mixes, substantial/dominant AI slices reported.  
   - Source: Ryan Law, [74% of New Webpages Include AI Content (Study of 900k Pages)](https://ahrefs.com/blog/what-percentage-of-new-content-is-ai-generated), Ahrefs blog.  
   - **Caveat:** Detector accuracy limits acknowledged by Ahrefs; not peer review; **not** “blogs only” — general new pages.

2. **Semrush — SEO ranking study (2025)**  
   - Analysis framed around **42,000 blog posts** / **20,000 keywords** and ranking positions; emphasizes **human-written pages outperform pure AI** in top positions (e.g., human ~80% vs. pure AI ~9% for position 1 in reporting summarized by trade press).  
   - Source (entry point): [Does AI content rank well in search?](https://www.semrush.com/blog/does-ai-content-rank-in-search-data-study/) (Semrush).  
   - **Caveat:** This is primarily **ranking performance**, not a clean prevalence census of “percent of all blog posts that are AI.”

3. **Workflow surveys (creation, not publication counts)**  
   - Ahrefs same article: **87%** of **879 surveyed content marketers** use AI to create or help create content.  
   - Semrush: **64%** of SEO teams describe **human-led AI-assisted** workflow (common model).  

### Confidence assessment — “40–60% of blog posts”

| Assessment | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|
| **Partially supported / needs rewrite** | Strong evidence that **AI assistance is default in professional content workflows** and that **most new web documents contain some AI-flagged text** (Ahrefs). **Not** the same as “40–60% of blog posts globally are AI-generated” without defining detector, corpus, and “AI-assisted.” |

**Recommendation:** Prefer **Ahrefs 74% of new pages contain some AI-detected content (English, Apr 2025)** with detector caveat, **or** cite **workflow adoption** (87% marketers use AI) **separately** from **prevalence**.

---

## 3. Synthetic social media accounts / content farms (scale, responses)

### NewsGuard — AI “content farm” websites (news-shaped sites)

- NewsGuard reports a tracked set of **AI content farm** sites in the **thousands**, with **rapid growth** (e.g., press materials describe **real-time detection datastream** and large running totals; public “AI tracking center” materials reference **3,006** sites in one 2025–2026-era summary encountered in secondary reporting).  
- Sources (organization hub): [NewsGuard AI insights](https://www.newsguardtech.com/insights/watch-out-ai-news-sites-are-on-the-rise); [AI tracking center](http://newsguardtech.com/special-reports/ai-tracking-center) (verify current count on live page before publishing externally).

### EU DisinfoLab — coordinated inauthentic behavior + generative AI

- EU DisinfoLab documents large **information operations** (e.g., **Doppelganger**) using **fake media properties** and scaled social dissemination; updates discuss **generative AI** in deceptive video and **AI-managed** account behaviors.  
- Source: [Doppelganger hub](https://www.disinfo.eu/doppelganger-operation); [Impact-Risk Index — AI and CIB](https://www.disinfo.eu/publications/updating-the-eu-disinfolab-impact-risk-index-addressing-ai-and-coordinated-inauthentic-behavior).

### Confidence — “millions of synthetic accounts … indistinguishable”

| Assessment | Notes |
|------------|-------|
| **Partially supported** | **Scale:** Strong evidence of **mass coordinated fakery** and **thousands of AI-dominated “news” domains**. **“Millions”** of bots/synthetics is plausible at internet scale but **should not be cited without a specific primary metric** (platform transparency reports, Botometer-style studies, or enforcement disclosures). |

---

## 4. Human ability to distinguish AI-written text (controlled vs. self-report)

### Multimodal / “realistic” synthetic media — near chance

- **Cooke, Edwards, Barkoff, Kelly.** “As Good As A Coin Toss: Human detection of AI-generated images, videos, audio, and audiovisual stimuli.” *Communications of the ACM* **68** (2025): 100–109. **DOI:** [10.1145/3729417](https://doi.org/10.1145/3729417). Preprint: [arXiv:2403.16760](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.16760).  
- **Key finding (from abstract):** n=**1,276**; mean detection performance **close to 50% (chance)** across modalities; several conditions worsen accuracy; prior self-reported knowledge did **not** significantly help; **age** did (older worse).  
- **Quote (arXiv abstract, paraphrased closely):** Results suggest it is **“no longer feasible to rely on the perceptual capabilities of people”** alone against weaponized synthetic media.

### Text-specific discrimination — better than chance on average, large individual differences

- **Human intelligence can safeguard against artificial intelligence: individual differences in the discernment of human from AI texts.** *Scientific Reports* (2024). **DOI:** [10.1038/s41598-024-76218-y](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-76218-y).  
- Reporting in secondary summaries: participants **above chance** on average; **fluid intelligence** predicts performance; heavier **smartphone/social media use** predicts **misattributing AI as human**.

### “Expert” annotators / heavy ChatGPT users — high accuracy in one ACL 2025 study

- **People who frequently use ChatGPT for writing tasks are accurate and robust detectors of AI-generated text** — ACL 2025 (Anthology [2025.acl-long.267](https://aclanthology.org/2025.acl-long.267)).  
- **Nuance:** Small pool of expert annotators; **does not** contradict weak average performance for general populations in other tasks.

### Confidence — “nearly impossible in 2–3 years without network verification”

| Assessment | Notes |
|------------|-------|
| **Partially supported as rhetoric, not as settled science** | Trajectory of model capability + economic incentives supports **risk framing**. **No** single peer-reviewed paper located that **proves** a 2–3 year timeline to “nearly impossible” for all users in all contexts. |

---

## 5. Growth rate / projections (2027–2030)

- **Empirical growth signals:** NewsGuard’s tracked **AI content-farm** count rising quickly year-over-year (verify live totals). Ahrefs describes **rapid embedding** of generative AI in default authoring tools, implying **continued upward pressure** on AI-touch rates in new documents.  
- **Doubling time:** No single canonical “doubling time for all AI web content” located; **avoid fabricated precision**.  
- **Confidence:** **Partially supported** for “rapid increase”; **Unverified** for specific doubling-time claims unless tied to a named dataset.

---

## 6. Search engines and platforms — quality signals and discoverability

- **Google:** Public guidance continues to emphasize **helpfulness**, **spam policies**, and **quality rater** concepts; trade coverage in 2024–2025 notes **quality rater guideline** updates referencing **generative AI** and **low-value** scaled content. Movemental should cite **official Google Search Central** pages for anything customer-facing.  
- **Semrush/Ahrefs findings (SEO industry):** Human-led pages still **disproportionately win top rankings** in reported samples—suggests **discoverability of genuine expertise is not yet fully “lost”** but **competition from scaled AI** is real.

### Confidence

| Topic | Assessment |
|-------|------------|
| “Signals are shifting” | **Verified (directionally)** — platforms publishing policies + detections + ranking studies. |
| “Genuine expertise undiscoverable” | **Contradicted as absolute** — ranking studies show human strong at top; **partially supported as risk** for mid/long-tail spam. |

---

## 7. “Credibility collapse” as academic concept

- **Named, unified phenomenon in one canonical paper (this pass):** Not identified.  
- **Adjacent concepts:** misinformation, epistemic fragmentation, trust in institutions (see Edelman materials), **E-E-A-T** / reputation in search, “dead internet” discourse (non-academic).  
- **Confidence:** Treat **“credibility collapse”** as **Movemental framing / synthesis**, not an established technical term—**define it explicitly** when used.

### Edelman (2025 materials) — AI trust

- **Edelman Trust Barometer** tech/AI streams and **flash poll** on AI trust (2025). Useful for **institutional trust** and **cross-national variation** (e.g., reported **low U.S. comfort** vs. some other regions in secondary summaries).  
- Entry: [2025 Edelman Trust Barometer](https://www.edelman.com/trust/2025/trust-barometer/); [Flash poll — Trust and Artificial Intelligence](https://www.edelman.com/trust/2025/trust-barometer/flash-poll-trust-artifical-intelligence).

---

## 8–9. Religious / nonprofit / movement leadership angle

### Theological education and publishing (2024)

- **ATLA Proceedings / theological librarianship:** Emerging literature on **AI in seminary classrooms**, **pedagogical and ethical** dimensions, and **AI policies** (e.g., proceedings entries on “AI in the Seminary Classroom”; *Theological Librarianship* special issues touching AI ethics).  
- Example entry points: [AI in the Seminary Classroom (Atla Proceedings)](https://serials.atla.com/proceedings/article/view/3561); [Theological Librarianship AI-themed volume (Oct 2024)](https://serials.atla.com/theolib/article/view/3512).

### Confidence

| Claim | Assessment |
|-------|------------|
| “Sector-specific prevalence data (what % of sermons / nonprofit newsletters are AI)” | **Unverified** in this pass — needs dedicated corpus study. |
| “Institutions are actively debating policies” | **Verified (low-stakes, qualitative)** via ATLA / seminary policy PDFs cited in secondary summaries. |

---

## 10. “Verification systems are being gamed at scale”

- **Evidence types:** documented **SEO spam networks**, **AI content farms**, **CIB**; academic work on **detector evasion** and **humanization** (not fully enumerated here after S2 API limit).  
- **Confidence:** **Partially supported** as a **directional risk** for *any* single-signal verification; **avoid overclaiming** without specifying *which* verification stack.

## Session limitations

- Semantic Scholar returned **429 Too Many Requests** during automated queries; literature list is **not exhaustive**.  
- Several statistics in SEO trade press were **not** independently re-crunched from raw CSVs in this session.
